summary: Rand Fishkin argues that the popular idea "those who can't do, teach" gets the causality backward — teaching or writing about a skill is often what makes you actually understand why you're good at it, not a consolation prize for people who can't do the thing itself. He references a New York Times piece making the same argument and describes his own experience: people who are excellent at something frequently never internalize the underlying "why" until they have to explain it to someone else in an accessible way. For him, writing about SEO and marketing for a broad audience forced him to extract the real mechanisms behind what worked, rather than just relying on intuition. The actionable takeaway is that if you want to truly master a skill you're already good at, forcing yourself to teach or write about it publicly is what surfaces the underlying logic you'd otherwise never articulate.
quote: I think that for many folks, you, uh, you learn vastly more about a topic when you have to teach it to someone else... if you're really good at doing something, you don't necessarily internalize why you're good at it or why it worked. And I think that writing about something in a way that's accessible to many other people is a way to internalize that lesson and to, uh, truly extract out the, the why of what happened.

summary: When an audience member asked about alternative marketing channels (guerrilla posters, radio, bus ads) after their paid Google/Facebook spend stopped working, Fishkin laid out why that happens structurally: whichever channel becomes the default place "everyone" does marketing gets flooded with capital, which drives ROI down toward the raw cost of acquisition because every competitor bids the price up trying to buy the same attention. Venture-backed companies make this worse because they'll knowingly sacrifice profitable growth for raw growth, overpaying in hot channels just to hit growth targets, which further inflates the price for everyone else. His actionable conclusion is that it pays disproportionately to find channels where your specific audience is paying attention but competitors haven't thought to look, precisely because those channels aren't yet crowded with well-funded bidders.
quote: the traditional method is, you know, you get money, you throw it at, you pour it into Google and Facebook, uh, and because everyone else is pouring it into Google and Facebook, the ROI is very low because the cost is nearly what anyone could make off of it... it pays so heavily to go and try and find out, like, oh, my audience maybe is paying attention here also and I can reach here even though no one else in my competitive group is.

summary: Fishkin names podcast advertising as the single most underdeveloped marketing channel available right now, backed by a specific gap between audience size and monetization: roughly 22% of Americans had listened to multiple podcasts in the past month, but under 5% of podcasts carried any advertising at all. He attributes the gap to the absence of a standardized ad marketplace or bidding platform for podcasts, unlike search or social, which keeps sophisticated advertisers out even though the audience is enormous. For a marketer, this is a concrete, current signal that a large, supply-constrained audience channel exists before the tooling catches up and it becomes crowded.
quote: 22% of Americans listen to multiple podcasts in the last month, but something under 5% of podcasts have advertising... it's insane how unmonetizable they are for their creators because there's no standardized platform and there's no bidding marketplace and nobody's playing there.

summary: When investors asked why SparkToro raised $1.3M instead of a smaller amount given its low burn rate, Fishkin's answer was that he deliberately sized the round to avoid fundraising again for two to four years, because the fundraising process itself distorts business decisions. His reasoning: while a founder is raising or anticipating the next raise, they end up optimizing the business to impress investors rather than to serve customers — even though most investors themselves will say not to do that, founders do it anyway because the incentive is unavoidable while a raise is looming. The generalizable lesson is to size a raise (or bootstrap runway) specifically to buy enough time that you're not making product decisions with one eye on the next fundraising pitch.
quote: because I don't want to spend another day thinking about fundraising for the next two or three or four years... I think it forces you to have to optimize your business, not for customers, but for investors, which if you're trying to build a profitable long-term company is dumb.

summary: Fishkin describes a multi-year strategic mistake at Moz: after venture investors repeatedly passed on the company in 2009-2011, he spent years reshaping Moz's business around the specific reasons those investors gave for passing, including eliminating services revenue entirely to look like a pure-SaaS company. Years later, the market trend reversed and a blended software-plus-services model became the newly favored pattern among the same class of investors, meaning the changes he'd made specifically to satisfy an investor preference actively hurt the core business and were obsolete almost as soon as he'd made them. The lesson is that chasing a snapshot-in-time investor preference, rather than what's structurally right for the business, is a bet on a moving target that's likely to lose.
quote: I spent years at Moz, optimizing Moz's business for what I heard was the reasons Valley investors turned me down in 2009 and 2010 and 2011. And those were bad moves. Those were bad moves for the core of the company. Things like getting rid of services entirely so that we had zero services revenue at all. And then, of course, what happened five years later? Oh, now the new trend is a blended software and services.

summary: Explaining why SparkToro's seed round was sized at $1.3M rather than something larger, Fishkin notes that the round size itself functioned as a social-proof signal to investors: because $1.3M was a familiar, "normal" figure for a seed round at the time, it read as reasonable and avoided extra scrutiny, whereas asking for $3M would have made investors pause and question the ask independent of the underlying business case. The implication for founders is that round size isn't just a function of runway math — it's also a number that either matches or breaks investor pattern-matching for what's normal at a given stage, and an oversized ask invites skepticism on its own.
quote: I think the other thing that helped on that front was that the round size didn't feel unreasonable. Right? I think if I had said three million, a lot more people would have been like, whoa, wait a minute. That much money?... But 1.3 being sort of a round size that many folks were familiar with in the seed stage felt comfortable.

summary: Fishkin attributes Moz's stagnant (though still growing) revenue directly to a strategic mistake: rather than staying focused on SEO while the field of SEO software itself was growing faster than ever, Moz tried to broaden into being something other than a pure SEO tool. That opened the door for SEMrush and Ahrefs to take over the narrowly-focused SEO positioning Moz had originally owned, and both overtook Moz in the category. The warning for other founders is that diversifying away from your core category — even when it's internally framed as growing the business — can hand your original market to a competitor willing to stay narrowly focused on exactly what you abandoned.
quote: Moz basically lost out to two big competitors, SEMrush and Ahreps, because we took our eye off the SEO ball and tried to go broader and be something other than SEO.

summary: Asked where he sees undeveloped advertising channels, Fishkin names live events as one of the biggest, pointing out the irony that a room full of qualified, high-intent leads — attendees at the very Startup Grind event he's speaking at — sits mostly unmonetized by the businesses actually in the room. The audience sponsoring or attending the event isn't systematically treating the other attendees as a marketing channel, even though that room represents exactly the concentrated, capable-buyer audience most companies pay heavily to reach through other means. The implicit point for a founder is that companies already paying to be physically present at industry events are leaving value on the table by not using the room itself — the other attendees — as a direct lead channel.
quote: here is a room of highly capable people who need all sorts of goods and services and why are we all here, right? We're here to, like, try and make our businesses do better. So that's another big one.
